attrs.alt: Azure RBAC scope hierarchy from root management group to individual resource

content.text: What Is Scope in Azure RBAC

Scope is the boundary within which a role assignment applies. Every role assignment has a scope, expressed as an Azure resource ID path. When you assign a role at a scope, the principal gains that role's permissions at the scope and at every child scope beneath it — this is called inheritance. Azure evaluates all role assignments that reach a scope (directly or via inheritance) and unions their permissions.

The scope hierarchy has four levels: management groups, subscriptions, resource groups, and individual resources. Broader scopes reduce management overhead but widen access. Narrower scopes require more assignments to maintain but enforce least privilege more precisely.

# The four scope levels in Azure RBAC (from broadest to narrowest)

# 1. Tenant root / Management Group
$rootMgScope = '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<tenant-id>'
$corpMgScope = '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'

# 2. Subscription
$subScope = '/subscriptions/aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd'

# 3. Resource Group
$rgScope = '/subscriptions/aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg'

# 4. Resource (individual)
$vmScope = '/subscriptions/aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm01'

# Assign a role at subscription scope (inherited by all resource groups + resources)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope $subScope

# Assign at resource group scope (applies to everything in that RG only)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope $rgScope

# Assign at individual resource scope (narrowest, highest precision)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Contributor' `
  -Scope $vmScope

# List all assignments at a specific scope (not inherited)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $rgScope | Where-Object { $_.Scope -eq $rgScope }

A role assignment at a parent scope is automatically effective at all child scopes. A user with Reader at the subscription level can read every resource group and every resource within that subscription — without any additional assignments. Inheritance flows strictly downward; an assignment at a resource group does not affect sibling resource groups or the parent subscription.
You cannot block an inherited role assignment at a child scope using another role. RBAC only allows permissions to be added, not subtracted, through roles.
To restrict a principal who holds an inherited role within a specific child scope, use Azure Deny Assignments (available through Azure Blueprints or Azure Policy).
Multiple role assignments at different scopes are additive: a user with Reader at subscription and Contributor at a resource group has Contributor within that resource group.
When auditing access, always check for inherited assignments — Get-AzRoleAssignment without -Scope returns all assignments including inherited ones.

# Inheritance: a role assigned at subscription scope is effective at every child scope.
# You cannot remove inherited permissions at a child scope using another role.

# Example: User has Reader at subscription, Contributor at prod-app-rg
# Effective permissions in prod-app-rg = Reader (inherited) + Contributor (direct) = Contributor
# Effective permissions in prod-data-rg = Reader only (no direct assignment there)

# Get all role assignments that affect a resource group, including inherited ones
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ResourceGroupName 'prod-app-rg' -IncludeClassicAdministrators

# Distinguish direct vs inherited assignments
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ResourceGroupName 'prod-app-rg' | ForEach-Object {
    $isInherited = $_.Scope -ne '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg'
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        DisplayName = $_.DisplayName
        Role = $_.RoleDefinitionName
        Scope = $_.Scope
        Inherited = $isInherited
    }
}

# To override inheritance, grant a narrower role at the child scope.
# IMPORTANT: you are ADDING permissions, not replacing.
# To restrict a user who has Reader at subscription within one RG,
# you cannot 'remove' the inherited Reader. Use Deny Assignments instead.

# Check assignments at management group scope
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'

Inheritance cannot be blocked by adding another role. If a user has Owner at subscription scope, you cannot reduce their access in one resource group by assigning Reader there — they still have Owner via inheritance. Use Deny Assignments for true exclusions.

Management groups sit above subscriptions in the hierarchy and are the broadest RBAC scope below the tenant root. A role assigned at a management group level is inherited by every subscription within that group and every resource below. This is the right scope for organization-wide roles: a security team that needs read access across all subscriptions, or a platform team that manages shared networking.

# Management groups organize subscriptions into a hierarchy for policy and RBAC.
# A role assigned at a management group is inherited by all member subscriptions.

# Assign Owner to a service principal at management group scope
# (Requires elevated access — Global Admin must elevate first)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<sp-principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'

# Practical use: Security team gets Reader on the root management group
# They can read every resource in every subscription without per-subscription assignment
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<security-group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Security Reader' `
  -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-mg-id>'

# List all management groups in the tenant
Get-AzManagementGroup | Select-Object Name, DisplayName, ParentId

# Get all subscriptions under a management group
Get-AzManagementGroupSubscription -GroupId 'corp' | Select-Object DisplayName, Id

# Move a subscription into a management group
New-AzManagementGroupSubscription -GroupId 'corp' -SubscriptionId '<sub-id>'

Individual resource scope is the most precise assignment level. Use it when a principal needs access to exactly one resource — a specific Key Vault, a single storage account, or one virtual machine — and granting resource-group-level access would be too broad. Some Azure resource types also expose sub-resource scopes, such as individual blob containers within a storage account.

# Individual resource scope: most granular, avoids over-privileging
# Useful when a principal needs access to exactly one resource

# Grant Key Vault Secrets User on a single vault only
$kvId = (Get-AzKeyVault -VaultName 'prod-secrets-kv').ResourceId
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<managed-identity-principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope $kvId

# Grant Storage Blob Data Contributor on one storage account
$saId = (Get-AzStorageAccount -ResourceGroupName 'data-rg' -Name 'proddata01').Id
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' `
  -Scope $saId

# Grant access to a single container within a storage account (sub-resource scope)
$containerScope = "$saId/blobServices/default/containers/audit-logs"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<auditor-principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' `
  -Scope $containerScope

# List all assignments scoped to a specific resource
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $kvId | Where-Object { $_.Scope -eq $kvId }

The decision of which scope to use is a balance between operational overhead and least privilege. The more assignments you create, the harder they are to audit. The broader each assignment, the more access you grant than strictly necessary. The right scope depends on the use case:
Root management group (Reader or Security Reader)
Platform management group or dedicated subscriptions
Dedicated resource group within their subscription

# Recommended scope strategy by principal type:

# Platform/network team: Reader at root MG, Contributor in platform subscriptions
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $platformTeamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-id>'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $platformTeamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<platform-sub-id>'

# Application team: Contributor in their own resource group only
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $appTeamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<prod-sub>/resourceGroups/team-a-rg'

# Managed identity: Key Vault Secrets User on the specific vault it needs
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $miPrincipalId -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
    -Scope $kvResourceId

# CI/CD service principal: Contributor on deployment target RG only
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $pipelineSpId -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<prod-sub>/resourceGroups/prod-app-rg'

# Security/audit team: Security Reader at root MG (read-only everywhere)
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $secTeamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Security Reader' `
    -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-id>'

Deny assignments are the only mechanism in Azure that blocks access regardless of any role grant. Unlike NotActions (which subtracts from a role's own grants), a deny assignment at a scope prevents the listed operation for all principals except those explicitly excluded. You cannot create deny assignments directly — they are created by Azure Blueprints (when a blueprint applies resource locks) and by Azure Managed Applications.

# Deny Assignments block access regardless of any role grants.
# They are created by Azure Blueprints and Azure Managed Applications — not directly by users.
# However, you can view and understand them via PowerShell.

# List all deny assignments in a subscription
Get-AzDenyAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' | Format-List

# List deny assignments at a specific resource group
Get-AzDenyAssignment -ResourceGroupName 'managed-app-rg' | Select-Object DenyAssignmentName,
    Principals, ExcludePrincipals, Permissions, Scope

# Deny assignment structure example (from a Blueprint lock):
# DenyAssignmentName : Deny assignment '...' created by blueprint assignment 'prod-blueprint'
# Principals         : {All Principals}
# ExcludePrincipals  : {<blueprint-sp-object-id>}   <- Blueprint SP is exempt
# Permissions:
#   Actions     : *
#   NotActions  : Microsoft.Authorization/*, Microsoft.Blueprint/*
# Scope          : /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/locked-rg
# DoNotApplyToChildScopes: false

# Azure Policy can also create deny effects — these are evaluated as part of ARM request processing,
# not RBAC, but they serve a similar purpose of blocking operations regardless of role grants.
# View Policy assignments that have a deny effect:
Get-AzPolicyAssignment | Where-Object {
    (Get-AzPolicyDefinition -Id $_.PolicyDefinitionId).Properties.PolicyRule.then.effect -eq 'deny'
} | Select-Object Name, Scope

Within a single Entra ID tenant, Entra ID objects (users, groups, managed identities, service principals) can be assigned roles in any subscription that belongs to that tenant. There is no cross-tenant concept in standard RBAC — cross-tenant access requires either B2B guest accounts or Azure Lighthouse delegation. Within a tenant, a managed identity created in one subscription can be granted a role in a completely different subscription using the identity's Entra ID object ID.

# Cross-subscription access: a principal in Tenant A accessing resources in Tenant B
# requires either Guest account in Tenant B or Azure Lighthouse delegation.

# Within the same tenant, cross-subscription access uses normal RBAC at subscription scope.

# Scenario: shared services subscription hosts DNS, Key Vault, monitoring.
# App teams in prod-sub need Key Vault Secrets User in shared-sub.

# Assign the app's managed identity (from prod-sub) a role in shared-sub
$mi = Get-AzUserAssignedIdentity -ResourceGroupName 'identity-rg' -Name 'app-worker-mi' `
    -SubscriptionId '<prod-sub-id>'

# Switch to shared-sub context and assign the role
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId '<shared-sub-id>'
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $mi.PrincipalId `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<shared-sub>/resourceGroups/shared-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/shared-kv'

# The managed identity PrincipalId is the same GUID across all subscriptions in the tenant
# because managed identities are Entra ID objects, not per-subscription objects.

# List all cross-subscription assignments for an identity
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $mi.PrincipalId -AllowOrphanedRoleAssignment | Select-Object Scope

Managed identities are Entra ID objects with a single PrincipalId valid across all subscriptions in the tenant. When a VM in prod-sub needs secrets from shared-sub's Key Vault, assign the VM's managed identity the Key Vault Secrets User role at the specific vault scope in shared-sub.

Role assignments should be defined in IaC alongside the resources they protect. Bicep and Terraform both support role assignment resources. The scope is specified as part of the resource ID, and the guid() function provides a deterministic name so re-runs remain idempotent. Managing assignments in IaC gives you audit history, peer review, and the ability to detect drift.

When reviewing access, scope is a critical filter. Assignments at subscription or management group scope affect many more resources than assignments at resource group level. Periodically review assignments at broad scopes — especially Owner and Contributor at subscription or higher — to ensure least privilege is maintained.
Scope is the 'where' of every Azure RBAC role assignment. Key principles:
The four levels from broadest to narrowest: management group → subscription → resource group → resource.
Role assignments at a parent scope are automatically inherited by all child scopes — you cannot block inheritance with another role.
Start assignments at the narrowest scope that meets the requirement; widen only when management overhead becomes excessive.

Management group scope is the right level for security teams and platform engineering; resource group is right for application teams; individual resource is right for managed identities and single-purpose service principals.
Use Deny Assignments (via Azure Policy or Blueprints) when you need to truly block a permission inherited from a broader scope.
